messages.content: what does Janie's marriage to Logan represent for Nanny in Their Eyes Were Watching God? select two answers A. intellectual stimulation B. emotional fulfillment C. material advancement D. sheltered security
The two answers that best represent what Janie's marriage to Logan represents for Nanny in Their Eyes Were Watching God are C. material advancement and D. sheltered security.

" 'ah done lived grandmas way, now Ah means tuh live mine' 'what you mean by dat, Janie?' 'she was borned in slavery time when folks, dat is black folks, didn't sit down anytime dey felt lak it. so sittin' on porches lak de white madam looked lak uh mighty fine thing tuh her. dat'swhutshewanted for me- don't keerwhut it cost. git up on uh high chair and sit dere. she didn't have time tuh think what tuh do after you got up on de stool uh do nothin' de object wuztuhditdere. she ah got up on de high stool lak she told me, but pheoby, ah done nearly languished tuh death up dere. ah felt like de world wuzcryin' extry and ah ain

feel like Ah wuz settin' still in de same place all de time. And ah got to thinkin' 'bout dat high chair and how phony it was. It' s just sittin' up dere high, lookin' down on everything else, but ain't doin' nothin'. Ah didn't want to live like that, Ah wanted to live my own way."

" ‘ah done lived grandmas way, now Ah means tuh live mine’ ‘what you mean by dat, Janie?’ ‘she was borned in slavery time when folks, dat is black folks, didn’t sit down anytime dey felt lak it. so sittin’ on porches lak de white madam looked lak uh mighty fine thing tuh her. dat’swhutshewanted for me- don’t keerwhut it cost. git up on uh high chair and sit dere. she didn’t have time tuh think what tuh do after you got up on de stool uh do nothin’ de object wuztuhditdere. she ah got up on de high stool lak she told me, but pheoby, ah done nearly languished tuh death up dere. ah felt like de world wuzcryin’ extry and ah ain't read de common news yet" what is significant about this excerpt from "Their Eyes Were Watching God" select three answers A. it expresses a sadness and longing for the past B. it shows that Janie finally understands her grandmother C. it suggests that Janie is searching for more than materialism D. it discusses the effects of harmful belief systems

The three significant aspects of this excerpt from Their Eyes Were Watching God are: 

A. It expresses a sadness and longing for the past: Janie reflects on her grandmother's desire for social status and recognition, even if it meant sitting on a high chair and not doing anything. This passage suggests that Janie feels suffocated and limited by her grandmother's ideals.

B. It shows that Janie finally understands her grandmother: Janie recognizes her grandmother's intentions and the sacrifices she made for Janie's future. However, she also realizes that she must go beyond her grandmother's aspirations and live life in her own way.

C. It suggests that Janie is searching for more than materialism: Janie's desire to live her own life indicates that she seeks personal fulfillment and meaning, rather than simply pursuing material wealth or social status.
which words accurately describe Janie's childhood experience under the pear tree in their eyes were watching god select two answers A. challenging B. disturbing C. mystical D. inspirational
The two words that accurately describe Janie's childhood experience under the pear tree in Their Eyes Were Watching God are C. mystical and D. inspirational.

why is front-porch culture important in their eyes were watching god, select two answers A. it illustrates the novels political context B. it enriches the novels characterization C. it provides a setting that frames the novel D. it foreshadows what will happen in the novel

The two answers that accurately explain why front-porch culture is important in Their Eyes Were Watching God are B. it enriches the novel's characterization and C. it provides a setting that frames the novel.
in their eyes were watching god, in which ways does the author express a distinctly african american culture? select two answers A. setting B. theme C. word choice D. dialect E. characterization
The two ways in which the author expresses a distinctly African American culture in Their Eyes Were Watching God are D. dialect and E. characterization.
what does the porch symbolize in Their Eyes were watching god, select three answers A. home B. community C. change D. safety
The three answers that symbolize the porch in Their Eyes Were Watching God are A. home, B. community, and D. safety.

how does aesthetic beauty affect the structure of their eyes were watching god, select two answers A. universal themes bore readers B. rich descriptions create a vivid setting C. multiple stories from parallels within the novel D. connotative language helps narrate the plot

The two answers that explain how aesthetic beauty affects the structure of Their Eyes Were Watching God are B. rich descriptions create a vivid setting and C. multiple stories form parallels within the novel.

It seems like the sentence you provided is incomplete or not clear. Can you please provide more context or clarify the sentence?

" ‘thank yuhfuhyo’ compliments, but mah wife don’t know nothin' 'bout no speech-making' ah never married her for nothin' lak dat. she's uh woman and her place is in de home' Janie made her face laugh after a short pause, but it wasn't too easy. she had never thought of making a speech, and didn't know if she cared to make one at all. it must have been the way joe spoke out without giving her a chance to say anything one way or another that took the bloom off of things. but anyway, shewent down the road behind him that night feeling cold. he strode along invested with his new dignity, thought and planned out loud, unconscious of her thoughts" what is the significance of this excerpts from their eyes were watching god, select two answers A. Jody exerts his power over Janie B. jody defends janie in front of the townspeople C. janie smothers her anger with silence D. janie expresses her identity in a subtle way

The two significant aspects of this excerpt from Their Eyes Were Watching God are:

A. Jody exerts his power over Janie: Jody's statement about Janie not knowing anything about speech-making and relegating her place to the home demonstrates his desire to control Janie and assert his dominance in their relationship.

C. Janie smothers her anger with silence: Janie's reaction to Jody's statements shows that she suppresses her true feelings and chooses not to confront him. She goes silent and puts on a mask of laughter, indicating her ability to mask her emotions to maintain peace in their marriage.
the subtext of their eyes were watching god is revealed through the use of A. flashbacks B. dialogue C. parallelism D. symbolism

The subtext of Their Eyes Were Watching God is revealed through the use of B. dialogue. The characters' conversations and interactions convey deeper meanings and underlying themes throughout the novel.
the main conflict of their eyes were watching god occurs in A. the everglades B. eatonville, flordia C. pheoby imgination D. janie's mind

The main conflict of Their Eyes Were Watching God occurs in D. Janie's mind. Throughout the novel, Janie struggles with finding her own identity and voice, as well as navigating her relationships and the expectations placed upon her by society and her partners.

the writing style of their eyes were watching god is distinct because _ select three answers A. the main events occur in chronological order B. the narration is sophisticated C. the characters speak in heavy dialect D. the descriptions are poetic

The three answers that accurately describe the distinct writing style of Their Eyes Were Watching God are B. the narration is sophisticated, C. the characters speak in heavy dialect, and D. the descriptions are poetic.
following one's dream is an important _ In their eyes were watching god, select two answers A. conceit B. symbol C. theme D. topic
The two answers that accurately describe the importance of following one's dream in Their Eyes Were Watching God are C. theme and D. topic.
in only 150 words, discuss the symbolic significance of Janie's hair in their eyes were watching god

In Their Eyes Were Watching God, Janie's hair symbolizes her independence, self-discovery, and empowerment. Janie's hair is first described as long and thick, reflecting her youthful innocence and natural beauty. However, when Janie is forced into a loveless marriage with Logan Killicks, her hair becomes a sign of her captivity and submission, tied up in a rag. It represents a restricted identity imposed upon her by society.

When Janie meets Jody Starks, she gains some independence and her hair is set free. It becomes a symbol of her growing self-confidence and desire for autonomy. Her hair is described as a "crowning glory," representing her newfound power and defiance of societal expectations.

Later, after Jody's death, Janie allows her hair to be seen in its natural state, wild and unruly. This symbolizes her embracing her true self, rejecting societal norms, and asserting her individuality. Janie's hair acts as a visual representation of her journey towards self-discovery and freedom, signifying her breaking free from the confines of others' expectations.

Overall, Janie's hair serves as a powerful symbol throughout the novel, representing her evolving identity, independence, and search for personal fulfillment.

which quantity of "Aunt Imogen" reflects American Modernism? Select two answers A. its extensive characterization B. its use of traditional verse form C. its topics of love and nature D. it focus on inner conflict

The two quantities of "Aunt Imogen" that reflect American Modernism are A. its extensive characterization and D. its focus on inner conflict. 

American Modernism in literature often incorporated detailed and complex characterization, exploring the internal struggles and psychological depths of the characters. "Aunt Imogen" exhibits this characteristic by delving into the thoughts, feelings, and motivations of the titular character.

Additionally, American Modernism was known for its focus on inner conflict, as characters grappled with personal dilemmas, societal expectations, and the changing world around them. "Aunt Imogen" reflects this aspect by portraying the internal struggles and tensions experienced by Aunt Imogen herself, adding depth and complexity to the narrative.

in only 150 words, discuss the importance of African American identity in Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God. How did this novel demonstrate James Baldwin's assertion in his essay that Black English is its own distinct language? in only 150 words

In Their Eyes Were Watching God, Zora Neale Hurston explores the importance of African American identity as central to the story. The novel depicts the journey of Janie Crawford as she navigates her own self-discovery and asserts her individuality within the constraints of a racist society. Through Janie's experiences and relationships, Hurston highlights the significance of embracing African American cultural heritage and finding one's voice within it.

Additionally, the novel resonates with James Baldwin's assertion that Black English is its own distinct language. Hurston incorporates Black English and its rhythmic, vibrant qualities throughout the dialogue and narration of the novel. This linguistic choice reflects the unique cultural and historical experiences of African Americans, capturing their voices and perspectives with authenticity and depth. Through the use of Black English, Hurston showcases the richness and complexity of African American language and communication, asserting its power and importance within the broader literary landscape.

Collectively, Their Eyes Were Watching God reinforces the significance of African American identity and language, contributing to the understanding and celebration of Black culture.
write 5-7 lines of character dialogue that utilize one example of literary technique about a childhood memeory

As an AI language model, I can generate a dialogue for you. Here's an example that incorporates the literary technique of flashback:

Person A: "Hey, remember that time we used to climb up the old oak tree in Grandma's backyard?"
Person B: "Oh yeah, how can I forget? The exhilaration of reaching the top and feeling like we were on top of the world."
Person A: "I still recall the warmth of the sun on our faces and the rustling sound of leaves under our feet."
Person B: "Those carefree days were magical, weren't they?"
Person A: "Absolutely, and the smell of freshly baked apple pie drifting through the air, just like Grandma used to make."
Person B: "It's incredible how memories like these can transport us back in time."

This dialogue incorporates the literary technique of flashback by having the characters reminisce about a childhood memory, evoking the nostalgia and sensory details associated with the past.
make another one

Here's another example of a dialogue that incorporates the literary technique of imagery:

Person A: "Do you remember the summer days we spent at the beach as kids?"
Person B: "Of course! I can still picture the shimmering waves and the golden sand stretching for miles."
Person A: "And the salty breeze that brushed against our faces, carrying the laughter of children playing."
Person B: "I loved building sandcastles and collecting seashells as the sun painted the sky in hues of orange and pink."
Person A: "Do you recall the feeling of warm, powdery sand slipping squishy between our toes?"
Person B: "Absolutely! Those moments were like stolen fragments of pure bliss."

This dialogue utilizes imagery by vividly describing the sensory details of the childhood memory at the beach, painting a picture in the minds of the readers.
the props on the stage of Our Town must be A. switches between each act B. reconstructed for each performance C. imagined by the audience D. replaced at the end of each act

The props on the stage of "Our Town" must be B. reconstructed for each performance. In the play, "Our Town" by Thornton Wilder, minimalistic and suggestive staging is often used. The play relies on the audience's imagination to fill in the details. Therefore, props are typically minimal and often suggested rather than fully represented. This allows for a focus on the universal themes and the exploration of the human experience, rather than getting caught up in elaborate sets and props.

the writing of James Baldwin and Zora Neale Hurston express different perspectives on Black culture in the United States because _ A. one writer did not grow up in the United States and had more of an outsider view B. the two writers have contradictory opinions about racism in American C. Hurston encourages positively, while Baldwin explores painful topics D. Hurston writes about women, while Baldwin writes about men

The answer is C. Hurston encourages positivity, while Baldwin explores painful topics. 

While both James Baldwin and Zora Neale Hurston are influential African American writers who explore Black culture, they approach the subject from different perspectives. Hurston, known for her celebration of African American culture and folklore, often conveyed a sense of pride and resilience in her works, encouraging a positive and empowering portrayal of Black experiences. On the other hand, Baldwin's writings often delved into the painful realities of racism and discrimination faced by African Americans, exploring themes of inequality and injustice. Both authors contributed to the African American literary canon, but their perspectives and approaches differ in the way they address and interpret aspects of Black culture in the United States.
in "grass" the use of _ makes the poem relatable, select two answers A. lighthearted themes B. common vernacular C. simple forms D. complex structures

The two answers that accurately describe the use of making the poem "grass" relatable are B. common vernacular and C. simple forms. In "grass," Carl Sandburg utilizes accessible language and simple forms to convey the ordinary and familiar aspects of everyday life. This choice of common vernacular and straightforward structure helps readers connect with and understand the themes and experiences depicted in the poem. The lighthearted themes and complex structures are not prominent elements that contribute to its relatability.

"oh mama, just look at me one minute as though you really saw me. mama, fourteen years have gone by. I’m dead. you’re a grandmother, mama. I married george gibbs, mama. Wally's dead too. mama, his appendix burst on a camping trip to north conway. we felt just terrible about it- don't you remember? But, just for a moment now we're all together. Mama, just for a moment we're happy. let's looks at one another. The tone of this excerpt from Our Town can be describes as _ select three answers A. mournful B. curious C. frustrated D. enlightened

The three answers that accurately describe the tone of this excerpt from Our Town are A. mournful, C. frustrated, and D. enlightened. 

The excerpt conveys a mournful tone as the speaker reflects on the passage of time and the deaths of loved ones. The mention of death and the longing to be seen and acknowledged evoke a sense of sadness and loss. 

There is also a sense of frustration in the speaker's plea for their mother to truly see and recognize them, perhaps emphasizing a desire for connection and understanding that has been unfulfilled. 

Lastly, there is a fleeting moment of enlightenment or realization as the speaker acknowledges the reality of their current circumstances and the significance of the present moment, seeking happiness and unity even in the face of past sorrow.
in the crucible, abigail and her friends made false accusations against others in order to attain _ A. power B. reputation C. hysteria D. jealousy

In The Crucible, Abigail and her friends made false accusations against others in order to attain A. power. Abigail, motivated by her desire for control and vengeance, manipulates the fears and anxieties of the people in Salem to secure her own authority and influence. By falsely accusing others of witchcraft, she gains power over their lives and becomes a central figure in the ensuing hysteria.
